seo.title: Uttaradit Province, Thailand — Complete Travel Guide 2026
seo.metaDescription: Explore Uttaradit Province: famous Monthong durian, Laplae teak town, Sirikit Dam reservoir, Phu Soi Dao National Park, and ancient cave temples. Area: 7,839 km², 9 districts. Authentic northern Thailand off the beaten path.

overview: Uttaradit Province is a quietly compelling northern Thai province that rewards curious travelers willing to step off the main tourist circuit. Sitting at the geographical transition between the highlands of the north and the central plains, Uttaradit is best known across Thailand for two things: its extraordinary durian — the 'Long' and 'Monthong' varieties grown here are considered among the finest in the country — and the charming old teak town of Laplae, whose wooden shophouses line the Nan River in a scene largely unchanged for over a century. The Sirikit Dam, one of Thailand's largest, creates a vast emerald reservoir ringed by mountains that is genuinely scenic. Phu Soi Dao National Park provides highland wilderness with diverse flora and rewarding hiking, while ancient cave temples and important monasteries add a layer of spiritual heritage. For travelers who want northern Thailand without the Chiang Mai crowds, Uttaradit offers an authentic and unhurried alternative.
geography: Uttaradit covers 7,839 km² in lower northern Thailand, straddling the boundary between the mountainous north and the upper central plains. The Nan River runs through the province from north to south, and the massive Sirikit Dam reservoir — formed by damming the Nan River in the 1970s — dominates the northern portion of the province. Mountains rise steeply to the east and west, with Phu Soi Dao (1,820 meters) in the east forming the border with Loei and Phitsanulok provinces. Forests cover approximately 50% of the province, including both deciduous and mixed tropical evergreen types. The southern districts flatten into agricultural plains producing rice, teak, and the province's celebrated durian orchards.
highlights: Uttaradit durian — the 'Long' and 'Monthong' varieties grown here are among Thailand's most prized, making durian season (May–July) a pilgrimage for fruit lovers nationwide
Laplae old teak town — a remarkably preserved riverside community of century-old teak shophouses along the Nan River, one of the most atmospheric heritage streetscapes in the north
Sirikit Dam — one of Thailand's largest dams, impounding the Nan River to form a scenic 260 km² reservoir encircled by forested mountains, popular for boat trips and fishing
Phu Soi Dao National Park — a highland park reaching 1,820 meters with diverse flora including montane forest, alpine meadows, orchids, and hiking trails to dramatic viewpoints
Wat Phra Thaen Sila At — an ancient sacred cave temple housing a revered footprint of the Buddha carved into stone, one of the most important pilgrimage sites in the province
Wat Phra Fang — an important historic monastery and pilgrimage center with Lanna-style architecture and a serene forest setting
Uttaradit night market — the city's lively evening market where locals gather for street food, fresh durian in season, and handicrafts from the surrounding villages

bestTimeToVisit.peak: Nov–Feb: Cool dry season with temperatures of 17–30°C. Clear skies, comfortable conditions for hiking Phu Soi Dao and exploring Laplae. The province is quiet — far fewer tourists than other northern destinations.
bestTimeToVisit.shoulder: May–Jul: Durian season — the highlight of Uttaradit's culinary calendar. Monthong and Long durian ripen from late May through July, drawing Thai visitors from across the country. Temperatures are warm (30–35°C) but the spectacle is worth it. Early rains begin from June.
bestTimeToVisit.lowSeason: Aug–Oct: Full monsoon season with heavy afternoon rains. The reservoir and national park are lush and green. Phu Soi Dao trails can be slippery. Roads to remote areas may be affected. Accommodation prices are lowest.
bestTimeToVisit.recommendation: Visit November to February for the best hiking and sightseeing weather. Plan a separate trip in May–July if tasting Uttaradit's famous durian at source is a priority — Thai durian enthusiasts consider this a bucket-list food experience.

topAttractions.name: Laplae Old Teak Town
topAttractions.description: The most distinctive townscape in Uttaradit Province: a row of century-old teak wood shophouses lining the western bank of the Nan River in the small town of Laplae, approximately 40 km north of Uttaradit city. The buildings date from the late 19th and early 20th centuries, built during the peak of the teak logging era when Laplae was a prosperous trading post. Walking the riverside promenade past the amber-coloured wooden facades, overhanging balconies, and traditional shop fronts gives a rare glimpse of old northern Thailand that has survived largely intact. A small local museum documents the town's teak history.

topAttractions.name: Sirikit Dam & Reservoir
topAttractions.description: One of Thailand's largest dams, completed in 1974 and named after Queen Sirikit. The dam impounds the Nan River to create a vast reservoir of approximately 260 km², ringed by forested mountains in a dramatic highland setting. The dam itself is an impressive piece of engineering — 113 meters high and 810 meters long — and the visitor center explains its role in flood control, irrigation, and power generation. Long-tail boat trips across the reservoir offer excellent mountain scenery, and fishing is popular along its shores.
topAttractions.entranceFee: Free (visitor center and dam viewpoint). Boat trips: approximately 300–500 THB per boat

topAttractions.name: Phu Soi Dao National Park
topAttractions.description: A highland national park in eastern Uttaradit, straddling the border with Phitsanulok and Loei provinces at elevations up to 1,820 meters. The park encompasses montane evergreen forest, open alpine meadows, dramatic cliff viewpoints, and a remarkable diversity of flowering plants including numerous orchid species. The main hiking trail climbs to the summit plateau with panoramic views over three provinces. Wildlife includes serow, barking deer, and a wide variety of bird species. The park's highest areas are cool year-round and occasionally see frost in January.
topAttractions.entranceFee: 200 THB foreigners / 40 THB Thai nationals. Camping available

sources.sourceName: Thai National Parks — Phu Soi Dao

topAttractions.name: Wat Phra Thaen Sila At
topAttractions.description: One of Uttaradit's most important sacred sites: an ancient cave temple enshrining a revered Buddha footprint (Phra Thaen) carved in stone. The temple complex sits in a forested hillside with cave chambers housing Buddha images and shrine rooms. Pilgrims from across the north come to pay respects to the footprint, which is believed to date from the Sukhothai era. The surrounding forest and cave setting give the site an atmospheric, timeless quality distinct from more formal temple compounds.

topAttractions.name: Wat Phra Fang
topAttractions.description: A historically significant monastery and pilgrimage site in Uttaradit, featuring Lanna-style architecture in a peaceful forested setting. The temple houses important Buddha relics and is associated with Uttaradit's deep Buddhist heritage. The compound includes a striking chedi and beautifully decorated viharn with intricate woodcarving. The temple is an active monastic community and one of the province's most respected religious centers.

topAttractions.name: Uttaradit Durian Orchards
topAttractions.description: Uttaradit's 'Long' durian — a locally developed cultivar with thin skin, small seeds, and intensely fragrant, creamy flesh — is considered one of the finest in Thailand, rivaling Chanthaburi and Rayong varieties in prestige. The 'Monthong' (Golden Pillow) grown here is also highly regarded for its rich flavour and firm texture. During peak season (late May to July), roadside stalls and dedicated durian markets spring up across the province. Visiting an orchard allows travelers to taste fruit picked the same morning at its absolute peak ripeness.
topAttractions.entranceFee: Free to visit roadside markets. Orchard tours: approximately 100–200 THB per person, durian priced by weight

topAttractions.name: Uttaradit City Pillar Shrine (Lak Mueang)
topAttractions.description: The spiritual heart of Uttaradit city: the Lak Mueang (city pillar shrine) houses the sacred wooden post that, in Thai tradition, anchors the spiritual protection of the settlement. The shrine is an active center of local worship — particularly vibrant during the annual Lak Mueang festival — and offers insight into the animist and Brahmanical traditions that underpin Thai Buddhist practice. The pavilion is ornately decorated and set in a small garden in the city center.

localFood: Long durian — Uttaradit's own cultivar with thin skin, tiny seeds, and intensely rich, custardy flesh; available fresh from orchards and markets May–July; considered a premium variety even by Thai durian connoisseurs

Monthong durian — the 'Golden Pillow' variety grown in Uttaradit produces exceptionally creamy, mildly sweet flesh; among the finest Monthong in the country due to the province's highland soil and climate
Khao Soi — rich northern Thai coconut curry noodle soup with crispy fried noodles on top, served with pickled mustard greens, shallots, and lime; a staple at Uttaradit city eateries
Sai Ua — northern-style spiced pork sausage grilled over charcoal, packed with lemongrass, kaffir lime leaf, galangal, and red curry paste; sold at morning markets throughout the province
Nam Prik Ong — a hearty chili dip of minced pork, tomatoes, and dried chilies served with sticky rice, crispy pork crackling, and seasonal vegetables
Gaeng Hung Lay — slow-cooked Burmese-influenced pork belly curry with tamarind, ginger, and roasted peanuts; popular at local restaurants and village festivals
Kluay Khai thong — small local 'egg bananas' grown in the Sirikit reservoir area, known for their intense sweetness; sold at roadside stalls and the Uttaradit morning market
culture: Uttaradit sits at a historical crossroads: it was an important Sukhothai-era outpost and later a border province of the Lanna Kingdom, giving it a layered heritage that blends central and northern Thai traditions. The province is closely associated with King Taksin the Great — the monarch who reunified Thailand after the fall of Ayutthaya in 1767 was born here, and a major memorial shrine in Uttaradit city commemorates this connection. The teak logging industry shaped the province's economy and built the distinctive wooden townscapes still visible in Laplae, while the Nan River — the lifeblood of the province — has sustained rice farming, fishing communities, and riverside trade for centuries. Uttaradit people take quiet pride in their durian heritage: the annual Durian and Fruit Festival, held each June at the provincial grounds, draws Thais from across the country to sample and celebrate the province's agricultural bounty.
gettingThere: From Bangkok, the most comfortable option is the overnight train from Bang Sue Grand Station to Uttaradit Railway Station (approximately 7–8 hours; lower berth second class ~550 THB, first class ~1,100 THB). Direct VIP buses from Bangkok's Mo Chit terminal reach Uttaradit in 6–7 hours (350–500 THB). There is no commercial airport in Uttaradit; the nearest airports are Phitsanulok (approximately 90 km south, 1.5 hours by bus or minivan) and Sukhothai (approximately 100 km southwest). From Phitsanulok, frequent buses and minivans connect to Uttaradit city (1–1.5 hours, 80–120 THB). From Chiang Mai, buses run via Lampang and Phrae with a total journey time of approximately 5–6 hours.
gettingAround: Uttaradit city is small and manageable on foot or by bicycle (50–100 THB/day rental). Songthaews operate fixed routes within the city for 15–25 THB per trip, and tuk-tuks are available for short urban hops. For out-of-town attractions — Laplae (40 km north), Sirikit Dam (60 km north), Phu Soi Dao National Park (80 km east), and the cave temples — renting a motorbike (150–250 THB/day) or hiring a songthaew for the day (700–1,000 THB) gives the most flexibility. The road network is well paved to all major sights. Grab operates in Uttaradit city with limited coverage. A private driver can be arranged through hotels for full-day trips to the dam and national park.
travelTips: Plan your Uttaradit trip around durian season (late May to July) if you want to taste the famous Long and Monthong varieties at peak freshness — roadside stalls offer tastings before you buy
Laplae is 40 km from Uttaradit city — a half-day trip by motorbike along scenic riverside roads; combine with a visit to the Sirikit Dam for a full day out
Book Phu Soi Dao National Park camping in advance via the national parks website during the cool season (November–February) — the sunrise from the summit plateau is exceptional
Uttaradit's Lak Mueang festival (usually November) and Durian Festival (June) are the two best times to experience local culture; check dates before visiting
Carry cash throughout the province — card acceptance is limited to hotels and a handful of larger restaurants in Uttaradit city
The overnight train from Bangkok is the most scenic and comfortable option — request a window seat for the stretch through the northern hills around dawn

Uttaradit is an excellent base for combining a northern Thailand itinerary with Sukhothai (1.5 hours), Phitsanulok (1.5 hours), and Nan (2.5 hours) — all reachable as day trips or overnight excursions
Dress modestly at Wat Phra Thaen Sila At and Wat Phra Fang — both are active places of worship with conservative dress codes (covered shoulders and knees required)

sources.sourceName: Thai National Parks — Phu Soi Dao
